recommendation: Codify as a general mechanical/security-check design rule: when a check's severity is contested as too coarse (a scoped, low-blast-radius action tripping the same tier as a catastrophic one), the fix is to refine the check's grading by blast radius (scope + reversibility of the flagged action), never to add a ratify/allowlist/config bypass around the check. An in-repo override IS a new attack surface — it becomes the thing an attacker (or a careless future edit) targets to silently relax the check, and it decouples the escape hatch from the judgment that should gate it. This generalizes past this CR: applies to any mechanical audit engine that gates CI (destructive-command guards, secret scanners, permission checks) facing a false-positive or over-broad-severity complaint.
evidence: CR #149 E1 destructive-command severity: scoped `rm -f <named rel file>` regraded HIGH (was CRITICAL); catastrophic forms (rm -rf/-r, sudo rm, curl|sh, dd, mkfs, fork bomb, rm -f on glob/abs/home paths) stay CRITICAL — a 15/15 crisp, non-overlapping blast-radius discriminator, not a bypass list

CR #149 explicitly rejected a ratify/allowlist bypass for the one CI-flip backlog CRITICAL (universal-plugin's scoped rm -f), reasoning the bypass itself is an attack surface (see spec-gate ledger line seq2, plan decision 4)
the blast-radius refinement alone auto-cleared the sole real CRITICAL surfaced by turning the mechanical scan on over 81 previously-unscanned skills, with no exemption/override mechanism added
